The Story of Cowboy Crude

Howdy! I heard Mr Crude from the Middle East gave you folks a version of his story last month. Now it's my turn.

I'm called WTI, aka West Texas Intermediate. I'm light and sweet, unlike Mr Crude that is medium and sour. 

My close relative is Forties from the North Sea - that guy is also known as Brent simply because he typically sets the price of Dated Brent. His cousin, the REAL Brent Crude gets quite annoyed as this confusion!

So Brent and I have a love-hate relationship. We are physical oil and we are also paper benchmarks. That's hell of a lot confusing for many people. But what matters is I am super powerful in the market nowadays, to the point that I could even set the price of Dated Brent! (Take that, cousin Forties!)

Anyway the world is in trouble now. Mr Crude is still stuck, not much moving out from the Strait of Hormuz. I'm happily being exported out of the USGC at a rate of about 5 mbd, depending on who's the best bidder. Korea and Japan are doing a pretty good job at securing my lot to make up for whatever they are missing. 

But I'm hearing it's still not enough. Who cares? I'll just gallop my way and keep on winning. Based on Sparta's Arbs Radar, it looks like I'm still the cheapest barrel around in town.

Yours truly,
Cowboy Crude 

P/S: Just a friendly reminder I don't make much diesel and fuel oil like Mr Crude does.

Can't believe half of 2026 is already in the rearview mirror. Time for the month end and H1 review.

As my followers already know, I took a massive punch to the jaw in my crude book from mid May to mid June, puking back a huge chunk of my YTD P/L.

The year started out absolute money with monster gains in precious metals and copper. I even built a massive long crude position during a sleepless night in Tel Aviv right before the Iran war kicked off.

From there, I rode the prompt futures rollover train. It was pure alpha through the April, May, and June Brent expiries, but the July contract broke me.

I tried to pivot out to the long dateds and structured options, but the sheer velocity of the crude selloff blew right past my max MDD. I had to rip the band aid off, cut the positions, and hand back roughly 70% of my cumulative H1 oil profits.

My macro framework on crude is still a total mess of conflicting signals:

First, can China really keep up this charade forever? Yeah, I get the consensus take that they have massive buffers and are just burning down excess inventory. But their energy security bottleneck remains a glaring geopolitical Achilles' heel. Extrapolating inventory baselines from years ago is a broken thesis—the reality is that China’s baseline crude appetite has structurally expanded.

Second, if you do the napkin math, global aggregate inventories are sitting below pre-war levels despite the historic SPR dumps. This whole episode just proved how massive the SPR weapon is, which means they’ll have to reload the clip eventually. So what happens to the tape when that structural bid hits the market?

Third, why on earth is Beijing running a playbook that deepens their own domestic deflationary pain just to bail out the American consumer? I’m starting to think this isn't an economic trade at all—it smells like a structural play to lock down state control over the domestic refining complex, or a full-blown dress rehearsal for a Taiwan contingency.

Fourth, getting your frozen funds back and dodging sanctions is a sweet deal. For Tehran, securing that without giving up an inch on their proxies or missile tech is a technical win. But Iran had the leverage to push Trump to the absolute brink and force a blowout midterm defeat, teaching the world a permanent lesson about trying to invade them. Why leave all that leverage on the floor?

I have a million other questions, but ending the final month of the half deep in the red tells me everything I need to know—I completely lost my feel for the oil tape. The only discipline that matters here is checking my ego, admitting I'm out of sync, and staying flat crude.

Since mid June, I aggressively loaded into a short EURUSD book, which helped patch up some of the bleeding from the crude disaster. The thesis was clean: the Eurozone's macro fundamentals are completely hollowed out, meaning ECB tightening would just trigger growth panics rather than the textbook rate hike currency bump, chocking out the Euro.

Luckily the FOMC accelerated the flush ahead of schedule, and I hit the bid to lock in profits. There might be more downside left in the tank, but I closed out the entire book last week bc I felt the market had priced in an overly aggressive Fed path that was stretching reality.

Right now, I’ve layered into some precious metals longs and used Monday’s flush to buy Nikkei futures. I’m not calling the absolute bottom in gold, and I know the immediate tape is facing downside pressure.

But all this panic about the new Fed Chair Warsh being a rabid hawk feels like a narrative that’s going to run out of gas. The market has just run way too far ahead of the data.

I understand the structural case for curve flattening, but even if the US economy keeps printing hot, inflation data is highly likely to blow up this hawkish path. Right now, the 'Transitory' playbook still looks like the higher-probability trade.

So, while I don't know where the absolute floor for gold is, trying to time it perfectly is a loser's game. With absolute doom-posting flooding the space and the weak hands getting completely rinsed out, this looks like a prime window to accumulate long exposure on the cheap. 

The structural long-term trend is ironclad. I’m looking to layer into this setup slowly, keeping a worst-case flush down to 3,500 on my radar. If this plays out right, it could turn into a massive print.

The Nikkei is a purely tactical, fast-money trade. The KOSPI is delivering absolute garbage vol right now—a total untradable tape. 

Japan has a fundamental story with solid momentum, and I’m going to keep riding the index until JPY vol wakes up and breaks the setup. Long-term, I’m still looking to camp out in individual Japanese equity names.

Honestly the current board feels pretty barren, which sucks. The real alpha right now should be in stock-picking and sector rotation, but my equity book is strictly locked down for long-term investing. 

H1 started with a bang and ended with a whimper, but we reset and look for cleaner execution in H2.

Good luck out there to the whole desk, and enjoy the Fourth of July weekend!

text:
据高盛判断，全球石油接下来会重新转向供应过剩，两个原因：1、伊朗战争对油市的冲击正在消退。2、霍尔木兹海峡的航运流量也在恢复。 高盛这个判断，正确概率多大？我的判断65%~70%。

第一，“战争溢价消退”，正确概率很高，大约 75%—85%，这个已经在价格和航运数据里发生了，路透调查显示，31位经济学家/分析师，已经把2026年布油均价预期从上月的 90.44美元 下调到 84.50美元，原因就是霍尔木兹海峡重开、供应中断担忧缓解。路透还提到，布油已经从此前 126美元以上 的高点大幅回落。

第二，“油市重新转向供应过剩”，方向应该是成立的，但时间点没那么稳，概率大约 60%~70%。

核心证据就是，需求明显被高油价和供应扰动打伤了，IEA 6月报告把2026年全球石油，需求预测下调为同比下降110万桶/日，同时预计2027年供应，会暴增约 800万桶/日，而需求只增加约 200万桶/日，这就是典型的过剩结构。

 EIA也预计2026年全球石油需求将下降 110万桶/日，相比此前“增长”的预测明显转空。 Rystad 更直接，认为全球原油市场可能在7月重新进入过剩，并预测2027年可能出现 500万桶/日 级别的过剩。

不过，“马上过剩”这个说法，我会打折，概率只有 50%~55%。

因为霍尔木兹不是开了就等于完全正常。路透航运跟踪显示，虽然油轮正在重新进入海湾，船东和租船方也在提前布局，但实际装载出口流量仍然大约只有危机前的一半，安全、保险、航道清理和拥堵问题都还没完全解决。 IEA也提醒，虽然美伊临时协议为中东出口恢复铺路，但排雷、通行安排和政治限制仍然会拖慢完全恢复。

所以我的结论是，高盛这次“方向判断”大概率是对的，但“节奏判断”存在风险。

油市最可能的路径是，先去战争溢价 → 油价继续回落或震荡下行 → 库存从极低位开始修复 → 到2026年四季度或2027年更明显转向过剩。

但短期内不能忽视一个问题，库存被抽得太狠了。 IEA称，战争以来全球可观察库存平均以 380万桶/日 的速度下降，5月单月库存下降 1.43亿桶。这意味着即使供应恢复，市场也可能先经历一段“补库存需求”，不一定马上崩。

总结一下，我感觉伊朗战争溢价继续消退80%，霍尔木兹流量继续恢复，但不完全顺畅70%，2026年下半年油市从短缺转向小幅过剩60%，2027年出现明显供应过剩70%~75%油价马上单边暴跌不到50%

对交易来说，高盛这个观点偏利空油价、利空传统能源股，利好航空、化工、消费和通胀回落交易。但如果霍尔木兹恢复反复、保险费不降、OPEC+主动限产，油价可能不会按高盛设想那么顺地下去。

一句话,高盛看对“大方向”的概率偏高，大概三分之二；但短线别把它理解成“油价马上崩”，更像是战争溢价退潮后，市场重新回到供应过剩叙事。

A Journey of Courage, Resilience & Safe Arrival at Paradip Port

Today, MT SANMAR HERALD safely arrived at Paradip Port and berthed at SPM-I at 11:30 hrs on 01 July 2026, marking the successful completion of an extraordinary voyage through one of the most challenging geopolitical situations in recent history.

After loading Basrah Medium and Basrah Heavy crude oil in Iraq, the vessel encountered the outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East. Operating under continuous coordination with the Indian Navy, the vessel endured prolonged delays, an attempted Strait of Hormuz transit, and even sustained shrapnel damage to its bridge during a hostile engagement. Throughout the ordeal, the Master and crew displayed exceptional professionalism, resilience, and commitment to safety.

With the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, the vessel successfully resumed her voyage and reached Paradip safely. Most importantly, all officers and crew members remained safe throughout the mission.

On arrival, the Master and crew were warmly welcomed by representatives of Paradip Port Authority and SERPL, including the Harbour Master, with bouquets and mementoes in recognition of their courage and dedication.

Paradip Port salutes the unwavering spirit of seafarers, whose dedication keeps global trade moving even in the most challenging circumstances.

⚓ Wishing the Master and crew of MT SANMAR HERALD fair winds and safe voyages ahead.

Oil prices rose on Wednesday as uncertainty over the US-Iran ceasefire increased following reports that Tehran would not hold direct talks with President Donald Trump’s envoys in Doha. Brent crude gained 0.69% to $73.45 a barrel, while WTI crude climbed 0.91% to $70.13 a barrel. Prices were also supported by a reported 6.1 million-barrel decline in US crude inventories last week.

Why oil prices are rising drastically?

Brent crude: +$0.33 (+0.45%) to $73.28/barrel
WTI crude: +$0.34 (+0.49%) to $69.84/barrel

The main driver is renewed uncertainty over Iran-U.S. peace talks. Markets fear that if negotiations fail, tensions could continue to threaten oil supplies from the Middle East.

1. Iran-U.S. talks hit a roadblock (Bullish for oil)
High-level U.S. officials traveled to Doha, but direct talks with Iran did not take place.
Instead, negotiations are happening through mediators.
Investors worry the conflict could drag on, keeping supply risks elevated.

2. Strait of Hormuz is improving, but risks remain (Mixed)
Shipping through the Strait is gradually recovering.
However, analysts say the situation remains uneven and unpredictable.
Any new disruption could quickly push oil prices higher.

3. Supply concerns are easing compared to earlier (Bearish medium-term)
Oil had already fallen sharply after hopes of peace reduced fears of supply disruptions.
Reuters' latest poll shows analysts have lowered their 2026 oil price forecasts, expecting supply risks to continue easing if stability returns.

4. U.S. crude inventories dropped sharply (Bullish)
API reported:
Crude oil inventories: -6.1 million barrels
Gasoline inventories also declined.
Lower inventories generally indicate stronger demand or tighter supply, supporting higher prices.

5. Official inventory data ahead
The market is waiting for the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) report.

If it confirms a large inventory draw, oil prices could gain further.

Market Impact
Bullish Factors ✅

Breakdown in Iran-U.S. negotiations.

Continued geopolitical risk in the Middle East.

Large decline in U.S. crude inventories.

Ongoing uncertainty around the Strait of Hormuz.
Bearish Factors ❌
Tanker traffic is recovering.
No confirmed closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Analysts have lowered long-term oil price forecasts.

If diplomacy resumes, the geopolitical risk premium could fade.
Overall Sentiment
Short-term: Bullish 📈
Geopolitical uncertainty and falling U.S. inventories are supporting oil prices.
Medium-term: Neutral to Bearish ↔️📉
If shipping through the Strait of Hormuz continues to normalize and diplomatic progress resumes, the geopolitical premium in oil prices is likely to diminish.

What traders should watch next
Any announcement on Iran-U.S. negotiations.
Updates on Strait of Hormuz shipping activity.
The EIA crude inventory report.

Any escalation or de-escalation of military tensions in the Middle East.
Bottom line: This is a headline-driven rally, not a fundamental supply shortage. Oil is rising because traders are pricing in geopolitical risk, while longer-term expectations remain relatively subdued unless the conflict escalates further.

The United States once came frighteningly close to invading Saudi Arabia; not during the Gulf War or after 9/11, but during the 1973 oil crisis.

When Arab OPEC members, led by Saudi Arabia, slapped an oil embargo on the U.S. and its allies for supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War, global oil prices quadrupled. America faced gas shortages, rationing, and long lines at pumps. Washington wasn’t about to let a foreign kingdom cripple its economy.

Declassified documents show senior officials in the Nixon administration and the Pentagon seriously discussed contingency plans to seize Saudi oil fields by military force if the embargo continued. The goal was simple: secure the oil America needed, by any means necessary.

The plans were never executed, but the message was crystal clear. Saudi Arabia got the memo loud and clear: cross the United States too far, and your oil fields could be taken.

Fearing U.S. military action, Saudi Arabia quickly came to the negotiating table for a bargain.

There was never any real “bargain between equals” though. This was classic carrot-and-stick diplomacy backed by overwhelming American power. Fear of invasion and U.S. military superiority forced Saudi Arabia into a lopsided deal.

Here's the deal;

In exchange for American protection and weapons, Saudi Arabia anchored the dollar in global oil trade (petrodollar system), recycled its oil revenues into U.S. Treasuries, bought massive amounts of American arms, and became a key strategic partner in the Gulf. The United States got reinforced dollar dominance, financial recycling, a permanent foothold in the region, and a compliant ally. Saudi Arabia got security guarantees it desperately needed in a hostile neighborhood.

The power imbalance was always obvious. One side strengthened its global currency, financial leverage, and strategic position. The other accepted terms shaped under the shadow of potential American military action.

The durability of that bargain has since been tested. On September 14, 2019, drones and cruise missiles struck Saudi Aramco's Abqaiq processing facility and the Khurais oil field, temporarily knocking out about 5.7 million barrels of daily oil production--roughly half of Saudi Arabia's output at the time. Washington blamed Iran for the attack, deployed additional troops and air-defense assets to Saudi Arabia, and tightened sanctions. But it stopped short of direct military retaliation against Iran. For the kingdom, the episode exposed an uncomfortable reality: even one of America's closest security partners could suffer a major strategic attack without triggering the kind of military response many had expected.

The 2026 war with Iran raised even deeper questions. Iranian missiles and drones targeted Saudi territory and energy infrastructure as the regional conflict expanded, while Riyadh focused on limiting escalation, protecting its economy, and preventing a wider regional war. Rather than relying solely on American protection, Saudi Arabia increasingly pursued diplomacy with Iran through Pakistan, diversified its security relationships, and sought to reduce its exposure to future conflicts. For a partnership built on security, the events of 2019 and 2026 forced a difficult question: what is the value of a security guarantee if it cannot fully shield you when the missiles start flying?

History often focuses on the agreements that were signed, it pays less attention to the pressures that shaped them. If Washington had never considered using military force in 1973, would the U.S.-Saudi relationship have evolved into the same bargain or did the shadow of American power influence the terms from the very beginning?

There are three ways to control the price of oil.

The nice way — through an OPEC agreement.
The ugly way — through military conflicts.
The Trump way — through paper shorting.
